claim: Context engineering—what goes into an agent's context window—is now more important than model improvements for enabling long-horizon agent performance.
actionable: When building agents, focus on optimizing what information is in context (traces, instructions, file system state) rather than waiting for better models.
source_title: Context Engineering Our Way to Long-Horizon Agents: LangChain's Harrison Chase

claim: The core algorithm of modern long-horizon agents—LLM running in a loop orchestrating its own context pulling—finally works due to both better models and better harness engineering, not just model improvements.
actionable: Understand that agent success depends on three things: model quality, harness design (planning, compaction, tool configuration), and context engineering practices.
source_title: Context Engineering Our Way to Long-Horizon Agents: LangChain's Harrison Chase

claim: Traces are the new source of truth for agent debugging and development, replacing code-based inspection; developers must examine traces from the beginning rather than adding them later.
actionable: Build comprehensive tracing into agent systems from day one; use traces (not code inspection alone) as the primary artifact for debugging, testing, and iteration.
source_title: Context Engineering Our Way to Long-Horizon Agents: LangChain's Harrison Chase

claim: Coding agents are leading all other agent applications because models are trained heavily on bash/file editing tools; coding becomes the lingua franca for general-purpose agent capabilities.
actionable: Recognize that coding agents are the beachhead for broader agent capabilities; many non-coding tasks may benefit from expressing them as code generation tasks.
source_title: Context Engineering Our Way to Long-Horizon Agents: LangChain's Harrison Chase

claim: File systems (real or virtual) are fundamental to long-horizon agent design for context management: summarization, tool result storage, and state tracking make agents far more capable.
actionable: Design agents with file system access (real or virtual via database); use files for compaction, storing large tool results, and maintaining state across long runs.
source_title: Context Engineering Our Way to Long-Horizon Agents: LangChain's Harrison Chase

claim: Building agents differs fundamentally from building software: agent behavior is non-deterministic (driven by the model), so you cannot know what the agent will do before running it.
actionable: Accept that agent development requires online testing and observational iteration; offline code review alone cannot verify behavior—you must run and trace.
source_title: Context Engineering Our Way to Long-Horizon Agents: LangChain's Anniversary Episode

claim: Compaction (context compression) is becoming a critical harness primitive; strategies include summarization stored in files and pushing large tool results to storage rather than the context window.
actionable: Implement compaction strategies in agent harnesses: summarize old messages to files, store large tool outputs externally, retrieve only when needed.
source_title: Context Engineering Our Way to Long-Horizon Agents: LangChain's Harrison Chase

claim: Sub-agents in orchestrated systems require explicit prompting to pass their work back to parent agents; failure modes occur when sub-agents do work but parent agents cannot see the results.
actionable: When using sub-agents, explicitly instruct them to format and return results in a way the parent agent can access—passing work through context, not just as internal state.
source_title: Context Engineering Our Way to Long-Horizon Agents: LangChain's Harrison Chase

claim: Building agents is more iterative than building software because you don't know the agent's behavior before deployment; you must observe traces, get feedback, and refine instructions repeatedly.
actionable: Plan for continuous iteration on agent instructions and prompts; allocate more development time than traditional software for observational refinement.
source_title: Context Engineering Our Way to Long-Horizon Agents: LangChain's Harrison Chase

claim: Memory systems that allow agents to learn from past interactions and refine their own instructions reduce the iteration burden on developers.
actionable: Implement agent memory that reflects on past traces and updates instructions; this enables agents to improve themselves rather than requiring constant manual refinement.
source_title: Context Engineering Our Way to Long-Horizon Agents: LangChain's Harrison Chase

claim: LLM-as-judge with human alignment calibration can replace pure rule-based evaluation; alignment evals in LangSmith train judges against human-annotated traces to match human preferences.
actionable: Build LLM judges by having humans annotate sample traces, then use those annotations to calibrate an evaluator model that aligns with human judgment.
source_title: Context Engineering Our Way to Long-Horizon Agents: LangChain's Harrison Chase

claim: Coding agents can access traces and diagnose their own errors via CLI tools, then use those traces to fix the codebase—enabling recursive self-improvement loops.
actionable: Expose agent tracing APIs (via MCP or CLI) so agents can pull historical traces, analyze failures, and generate fixes in their own codebase.
source_title: Context Engineering Our Way to Long-Horizon Agents: LangChain's Harrison Chase
